coaching.title: Today's Run
coaching.message: Saturday long run: 14–16 miles with 1,200–1,400 ft of vert, Z2 aerobic effort (130–138 bpm). You're in taper week 21, so volume is intentionally reduced from peak (you ran 48.8 mi last week, now at 37.1 mi so far). Your recovery score of 52/100 is moderate—not suppressed enough to skip, but not license to surge. Keep the effort conversational and anchor to terrain that mirrors Kranjska Gora: sustained climbs on firm, rolling ground. This is a sharpness run, not a fitness run. Finish strong but not empty.

coaching.title: Fuel Right
coaching.message: You slept 6h 27m with 94.7% efficiency—solid sleep quality, but total duration is 36 minutes below your 30-day average. This signals either travel fatigue or accumulated stress. Eat 120–150g of carbs during the run (gels, sports drink, or real food depending on aid station availability on your route). Post-run within 30 min: 40–50g protein + 80–100g carbs to arrest muscle protein breakdown and replenish glycogen. Tonight, prioritize a high-carb dinner (rice, pasta, or potatoes) and aim for 8h of sleep—your body is in supercompensation phase and needs that recovery window.

coaching.title: Recovery
coaching.message: Resting HR is 63 bpm, 1.8 bpm above your 30-day average—a minor tick upward that warrants attention but isn't a red flag. After this long run, use an ice bath (10–15 min at 50–55°F) or cold shower to suppress DOMS and accelerate parasympathetic tone; your recovery score will thank you. Compression gear (calf sleeves, tights) for 2–3 hours post-run. Aim for 8+ hours tonight. Tomorrow is easy recovery (6–8 miles, Z1) with no vert—treat it as a flush day before you dial back further Monday–Wednesday.

coaching.title: Mental Edge
coaching.message: You're 76 days from Kranjska Gora and in the final taper stretch. The workload is lighter by design, but your mind may feel restless—that's normal and means fitness is intact. Use today's long run to practice race-day logistics: test your hydration bottle, fuel timing, and mental anchors on climbs. The Julian Alps demand patience on ascents and controlled descents; rehearse eating while moving uphill at moderate effort. Trust the taper. You've done the work. Now you're sharpening the tool.
